author: @martinduddridge329
text:
Way back in history as an apprentice, our apprentice training school took part in a Shell oils sponsored electric vrijval challenge. All vehicles had the same motors and the same lead acid batteries (it was 1980) we had 2 cars, one with electronic power control (the electrical apprentices) and one with relay control ours! (Mechanical apprentices) . Theirs offered smooth linear power control ours was stepped power control. During testing and the event the electronics proved fragile with many many appearances of the magic smoke, failing to finish the event, ours was reliable, power hungry due to the stepped power delivery but reliable and we got 5th out of 15. 
Thank god electronics have evolved since them!

author: @FuhrChris
text:
I'm having complications trying to contact you in private E-mail, so I'm sending you this.
I've seen Induction heater made from a rotating disk and rare earth magnets.  (These can be connected to a windmill) 
I've seen You tubes where a high speed Alu. Disk with Rare Earth Magnets was causing pieces of Alu. to melt in the area closest to contacting the Magnest, Copper rod got Red hot.
What I am curious about it making an adapted metal melt crucible to fit over the top of the spinning rare Earth magnets?
Option 1: Would placing like copper particles in an outer layer of a Graphite crucible work?
Or 
Option 2: place the Graphite crucible in a thick copper pan with 100% contact between the two?  (This option might work for metals that have a melt temp lower than copper goes soft?)
Or
Something else?

The Range of applications include:
Heating a chamber for Wax burn out in lost Wax casting (300 Degrees) The flasks are metal but the casting plaster would not directly take the heat.  Even distribution of the heat is very important.
Flasks have a hole at there bottoms and heating from just one side of the metal flask would create problems such as the too hot area would hit liquid wax first and be still trapped inside the mold (This results in cracks in the mold as the wax tries to get out when the exit is blocked).
(For a small one at a time system. Having two disks with one on each side with both of them in the vertical would mean that the wax would flow downwards and out of the mold) 
Larger batches would need to be done in a chamber so it would probably be a metal floor to the chamber, or heating a vat of water to the point of the steam Burn out system.

Melting Bismuth Metal.
I purchased at a going out of Business Auction a "White Metal spin caster" and a "Buzzer" melting pot (The Buzzer did not have a propane nozzle but does have an All metal melt pot that would work for Bismuth/Pewter metal casting.)(520 degrees)

It's when you get to the temps of higher than Bismuth Metal that you need things like a Graphite Crucible which can be made at home, and changed up to the needs of this application.
Silver (1763)
Small single flask pours of Silver are done with a coated ceramic dish on a metal long arm.  So ceramics are used sometimes.
And nobody is affording Gold anymore. 
For Everdure bronze, even getting a 30 pound Number 10 Salamander crucible part way up to temp would save a ton of propane.

*German Silver A.K.A. White Brass is another high temp melt but is still a little bit lower than pure copper.
(Because it is the most skin reactive Jewelry metal and only used in Costume Jewelry (OK. Maybe there is a market for Live action Roll Play in which Vampires would love to have a fake Silver Metal.)  There isn't much market for the Fake Metals like "Aztec Gold" and they require an acquired specialization skills in making a quality pour. 

Yes, I have high winds here, enough land for several windmills, and both the tools to re-open my bronze foundy and Jewelry lab, and I have the tools under tarps to build the Induction Heater.

Thank you for your possible considerations in advance of this problem/opportunity.

author: @kenham6742
text:
no.  Only half the time a relay can do what a transistor can do (with more power).
Why?  Because half the time, a transistor is used between on and off in an analog fashion (as with amplification) rather than a digital fashion (on or off).
You don't know that?  How do you not know that?  You have many years.  Please stop teaching people wrong.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's very rude of you mate - i talked about exactly that in the video!

replies.author: @kenham6742
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I certainly don't mean to be rude.  I admit the "Please stop teaching people wrong," was probably a bit much.  However, I still have a question:  Where (what time stamp) in the video do you mention transistors can't be replaced by relays, due to transistor's analog (amplifier) use?  After all, it was it's most important use at the time in 1947 by Bell Labs (for the telephone industry).  Analog was the purpose of it's invention ; it revolutionized global communication.  I didn't see it in the video.

replies.author: @kenham6742
replies.text: 2:38 in the video, you do mention transistor current "will vary to the same degree" for amplification, and that the relay would be "rubbish".  I missed this brief mention.

author: @VeniceInventors
text:
For those who are new to relays and intend on using them in critical applications, consider using two relays to provide a safe fallback in case of failure.
For example, by putting two relays in series, if one gets stuck in the on state, the other will still cut the power off. 
It's less likely for a relay to get stuck on rather than off, but an extra relay is much cheaper than the damage some failures can cause.

author: @TomFarrell-p9z
text: Interesting video as always.  Relays were used as "amplifiers" in telegraph circuits, long before the vacuum tube was invented, to extend the range of long distance lines.  The relays and a battery would be located (often beside telephone tracks) somewhere between the telegraph stations and the relays would switch power from the battery onto the outgoing line.  Obviously, in telegraphic communications the bang-bang nature of relays was ideal, with no need (or desire) for a linear response.

author: @mattmiller220
text:
The key thing you need in a solid state material to replicate the amplification properties of transistors is what’s sometimes called ‘negative resistance’. When you set up the differential equations for a closed circuit, an element with ‘negative resistance’ will enable solutions to have oscillatory behavior as well. If you can obtain oscillation from a DC current in a material, that’s a dead giveaway that your material has some transitor properties (including amplification). A bit hand wavy but something I learned a while back and it made a lot of sense to me that way, wanted to share.

But anyway, on relays: There are some good videos here on YT of people building small computers from many, many small relays. Looks cool. 😎

replies.author: @whatelseison8970
replies.text: The oscillatory behavior comes from the reactive components.  Without a tuned circuit negative resistance devices are usually bistable devices; swinging to one extreme and staying there.  Negative resistance, where it can be said to exist, is more like an animating force that drives the circuit away from equilibrium. Personally, I would argue negative resistance is mostly a useful concept wrt to devices like tunnel diodes, lambda diodes, or other similar 2-port devices.  When discussing 3 terminal devices like transistors as they are used in oscillators or amplifiers, it is usually possible to analyze parts of the circuit as though some negative resistance existed there, but usually it simply makes more sense to analyze the circuit holistically, in terms of positive feedback and impedance.

author: @GrandadTinkerer
text:
Hi Robert. Thanks for the video, as always.
Just a thought;
Maybe it would have been better to have the drawing of the two relays, shown as the 'schematic' representation. I.E. show the coil and all the contacts.
Probably easier to follow what's actually happening.
Not a criticism, just trying to put myself in the shoes of a beginner.

replies.author: @thekaxmax
replies.text:
There's a reason why some computerised control systems have a sound output, including one nuclear test facility. You can hear there's something wrong and ID it far faster than looking at and analysing readouts, and it's aved a lot of money and avoided injury.
Sci Fi (and real world) ship bridges and engineering control rooms should both have a low background hum of a reassuring frequency and faint beat at all times, noting a ship that's operating correctly. If it's off or sounds weird, everyone instantly knows that something is wrong, what it is, and how bad it is. Reaction times are _much_ faster.
Worth covering as an general idea.
